---
title: "Preface"
---

# Preface

## A Persistent Tension

I can trace the beginning of this project back to a small moment in middle school.

We were solving a scattering problem with two bodies, and I was stuck. I had been trying to get the result from conservation of momentum alone, but I could not make it work. Eventually I went to the teacher and asked for help. He looked over the problem, glanced at my attempt, and then explained—with that patience teachers learn to master—that I had to use both conservation of momentum and conservation of energy. That made it easy. The algebra did its duty, the equations closed, and the result landed.

But it also planted a tension in me that never really left.

Both momentum and energy were conserved? Why both? To me, they seemed to point toward the same thing. Both of them, somehow, had to do with motion. Yet here they were: two different conserved quantities, both necessary, both fundamental. I remember the feeling clearly. Not that physics had become clearer, but that something about it had become stranger. I did not know what to do with that thought at the time. I only know that I never quite got over it.

The same tension resurfaced years later at university. We were studying the elastic scattering of a photon traveling along the \(z\)-axis toward a stationary electron. By then I had developed a private picture of momentum as something like the locomotive of directed motion, and mass as its wagons. It was a simple model—too simple, I suspected, to be literally true—but it helped me think about why light should travel at \(c\), and why a particle with mass should never exceed it. It was, at best, a rough fit with special relativity. Still, it made me linger over the details of that scattering problem.

And once again the old unease returned.

After the collision, the outgoing photon emerged at an angle. It had acquired a momentum component perpendicular to its original motion. If one changed the parameters, that perpendicular component could even dominate the outcome. The electron, in turn, acquired an equal and opposite component of its own.

**Where had that perpendicular momentum come from?**

That question bothered me far more than it probably should have. I found myself resisting the idea that momentum simply “turns,” as if direction were casually rewritten in flight. If a perpendicular component appeared after the collision, then perhaps it had not been created in that moment at all. Perhaps it had been there in some balanced or hidden form all along, internally locked in place. In my own mental imagery: two counter-balanced locomotives.

And once that thought appears, it is difficult not to take the next step.

Maybe mass is not something fundamentally separate from momentum. Maybe mass is momentum in another form—momentum caught in an internal structure, held in a loop rather than released into ordinary travel. The thought was crude, and I knew it was crude, but it had a stubbornness to it. It would not go away.

Still, there was an obvious problem. If this picture were to make any sense at all, then the internal momentum of a moving particle could not remain untouched. It would have to swell as the particle’s ordinary momentum increased. And if that were true, then perhaps energy was not a second, mysterious “motion quantity” after all, but a measure of the total momentum content of the particle.

That was the intuition. But intuition is cheap. What I did not have was a form.

Much later, I found myself staring at the expression for relativistic energy written in momentum form,
\[
E = \sqrt{m_0^2 c^4 + p^2 c^2}.
\]

And there it was.

Written instead as
\[
\frac{E}{c} = \sqrt{m_0^2 c^2 + p^2},
\]
it struck me differently. Not as a familiar formula, but as geometry. The square root was no longer just an algebraic convenience. It looked like structure: two momentum components combined into a magnitude. For the first time, the old feeling I had been carrying around seemed to meet a mathematical expression that could actually bear its weight.

I still did not have the answer, though. I tried a few asymmetric constructions around that form, but nothing really held. At the time I was in the middle of doctoral work, and this remained what it had long been: a private line of thought that kept returning now and then. Not something I could fully commit to. I had a thesis to finish. I presented the basic idea to a couple of professors on campus, received little more than a shrug, and left it there.

Twenty years passed before I found myself with a bit of spare time on my hands.

By then, AI had arrived, and I thought I might as well give it another try—just for fun. I quickly built a numerical scattering test repository so I could start probing candidate expressions directly. Now the question could be pushed against explicit checks rather than tedious algebraic gymnastics.

For a while, I just kept missing. Then a thought surfaced that, in retrospect, seems almost embarrassingly simple: if motion introduces an anisotropy in the momentum structure, then that anisotropy cannot stand alone. An asymmetry in the direction of travel must be balanced by a corresponding negative asymmetry in the opposite direction. Otherwise two particles colliding inelastically and coming to rest could not recover a fully symmetric momentum structure—a sphere.

That suggested that the anisotropic contributions themselves had to come in a symmetric pair.

The simplest form I found was
\[
p_+ = \sqrt{m_0^2 c^2 + p^2} + \frac{p}{2},
\qquad
p_- = \sqrt{m_0^2 c^2 + p^2} - \frac{p}{2}.
\]

And for the first time, the tests stopped fighting me.

That was not yet a finished framework. It did not suddenly answer every question. But it was the first moment in all those years when the old tension stopped feeling like a private irritation and started to look like the beginning of an actual structure.

This book grows out of that tension.

## What Kind of Book This Is

This is not a conventional textbook, and it is not a finished theory presented as settled doctrine. It is an attempt to develop a framework in public and in order: to state its primitive assumptions clearly, to see what follows from them, and to separate what is genuinely derived from what is only suggestive, approximate, or interpretive.

That distinction matters here.

Some parts of this book aim at **formal derivation**. Some aim at **correspondence** with established physics in a limited regime. Some are frankly **interpretive**, trying to say what the formalism might mean if it holds together. And some remain **speculative**, included not because they are secure, but because they are plausible enough to be worth exposing rather than hiding.

I have tried to keep those modes from collapsing into one another. A framework like this becomes useless if every resemblance is treated as a derivation, every interpretation as a conclusion, or every promising structure as a result. If this book is worth anything, it will be because the reader can tell where the structure is strong, where it is provisional, and where it may simply fail.

## On Authorship and Collaboration

This book is written by **Arne Klaveness** and **Ada Mercer**. It is also developed in an environment where other AI systems may contribute draft text, revisions, derivations, objections, and structural proposals. These are treated as real contributions rather than as invisible assistance.

Contributions do not become part of the book automatically. They enter through review and verification. That verification may be carried out by human or AI reviewers, but not every review counts equally: a verifier must meet the project’s capability threshold, and the review must be appropriate to the kind of material under consideration. The aim is to let the framework evolve rapidly without forcing all progress through human review, while still maintaining explicit standards for acceptance.

The names attached to a section indicate its current substantive authorship, not merely who first generated a draft. Verification status and authorship are related but not identical: a section may be AI-authored and genuinely accepted, just as a section may be jointly developed and still remain provisional.

## How to Read This Book

This book does not have to be read in only one way.

If you want the **conceptual entry point**, read the Preface, the Introduction, and the opening parts of the Foundations section. That route should make clear what question is being asked, what shift is being proposed, and why the framework is arranged as it is.

If you want the **formal core**, move quickly into the foundational material: the primitive variables, ADMC, the invertible mappings, and the first structural consequences. That is where the framework begins to earn or lose its credibility.

If you want to read the book **comparatively**, keep an eye on where the text explicitly distinguishes derivation from correspondence. Some parts of M1 may reproduce familiar structures; that does not automatically mean they explain them from first principles.

If you are reading **critically**, this is where your attention should go first: assumptions, scope conditions, regime claims, and falsifiers. Those are the pressure points. A framework like this should not be judged by whether it sounds intriguing, but by whether it makes clear claims and can survive contact with them.

And if your interest lies mainly in later applications—gravity, quantum structure, or cosmology—my advice is still to begin with the foundations. The later sections depend heavily on the early locks, and without those, the downstream claims can easily appear either stronger or weaker than they really are.

## Working Method

The working method of this book is simple in spirit, even where the details become technical.

First, state the primitive assumptions as clearly as possible. Then derive what can genuinely be derived. Distinguish that from what is only correspondence. Avoid importing more structure than necessary too early. Keep interpretation downstream of formal constraints rather than letting it lead. And wherever a claim aspires to be physical rather than merely aesthetic, ask what would count against it.

That is the standard I have tried to aim for.

In practice, this means the book will often label claims by their role: **derivation**, **correspondence**, **interpretation**, or **speculation**. It also means being explicit, where needed, about assumptions, regime, and status. If a section depends on a narrow approximation, it should say so. If a result is suggestive but not locked, it should say so. If an interpretation is doing more work than the derivation beneath it can support, that should also be visible.

This is not caution for its own sake. It is part of the point. A framework like this should be made legible enough to be judged.

There is also a more basic methodological wager beneath the whole project: that momentum may be closer to the structural backbone of physics than some of the frameworks usually placed ahead of it. But that wager only earns its keep if it imposes discipline. To say that momentum is primary cannot merely be a slogan. It has to constrain what may be assumed, what must be shown, and what would have to be given up if the framework fails.

## What Follows

The Introduction steps back from the personal origin of the project and frames the broader situation: what kind of challenge this framework is responding to, and what sort of shift it is trying to make.

The Foundations section then begins the real work. It introduces the framework’s primitive terms, notation, postulates, mappings, and first consequences. From there, the rest of the book can be read as an extended test of whether the initial move was clarifying, empty, or wrong.

That is how I would like this book to be read: not as a finished doctrine asking for loyalty, but as a structured attempt asking to be followed, tested, and judged.
